name: Kui Xing
description: Kui Xing, also known as Da Kui Xing, is a Chinese deity associated with examinations, scholarship, and success in civil service examinations. He is often depicted as a grotesque figure, sometimes with a demonic appearance, reflecting the idea that even those who may seem unconventional or flawed can achieve great things through diligence and perseverance. Kui Xing is typically portrayed standing on one leg on a fish or turtle, holding a writing brush, and using it to mark the names of successful candidates on a list or scroll. His image is frequently venerated by students and scholars hoping to pass their examinations, particularly the imperial examinations of old. The deity's origins are somewhat obscure, with various legends attributing his appearance and role to different stories of failed scholars who, despite their physical imperfections or setbacks, ultimately achieved immortality or divine status. He serves as a symbol of hope and encouragement for those striving for academic excellence and career advancement.
subtitle: God of Examinations

keyMyths.title: The Failed Scholar
keyMyths.description: One popular myth recounts Kui Xing as a brilliant but physically unattractive scholar who failed to pass the imperial examinations due to his appearance. Distraught, he attempted suicide but was saved by a sea creature, often depicted as a turtle or fish. This experience transformed him, and he was elevated to divine status, becoming the patron deity of examinations, helping other scholars achieve the success that eluded him in life. This story emphasizes the importance of perseverance and the possibility of redemption, even in the face of adversity.
keyMyths.source: Werner, E.T.C. Myths and Legends of China. George G. Harrap & Co. 1922.

keyMyths.title: The Dipper Star Connection
keyMyths.description: Another interpretation connects Kui Xing with the Dipper Star (Ursa Major). Some scholars believe that his name is derived from the shape of the Dipper constellation, and his one-legged stance is seen as a representation of the constellation's unique arrangement. This connection suggests a cosmic significance to Kui Xing's role in regulating academic success, implying that fate and destiny play a role in determining who achieves scholarly recognition. The Dipper Star's association with navigation and guidance further reinforces Kui Xing's role as a guide for aspiring scholars.
keyMyths.source: Williams, C.A.S. Chinese Symbolism and Art Motives. Charles E. Tuttle Company. 1974.

keyMyths.title: Marking the Successful
keyMyths.description: The most common depiction of Kui Xing involves him standing on a fish or turtle and using his writing brush to mark the names of successful candidates on a list or scroll. This imagery underscores his active role in determining who passes the examinations and achieves official status. This myth highlights the importance of meritocracy and the belief that scholarly achievement is rewarded by divine intervention. It reinforces the idea that diligent study and preparation are essential for success in the competitive world of imperial examinations.
keyMyths.source: Christie, Anthony. Chinese Mythology. Hamlyn Publishing. 1968.
